2026-06-06T22:59:26.7578758Z [36;1mset -euo pipefail[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7579190Z [36;1m# actions/download-artifact (>=v5) flattens contents directly into `path/`[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7579724Z [36;1m# whenever exactly one artifact matches the pattern, ignoring[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7580227Z [36;1m# `merge-multiple: false`. Re-shape into the per-group subdir layout so[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7580775Z [36;1m# downstream validation sees a stable structure regardless of matrix size.[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7581192Z [36;1m# Upstream reference:[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7581646Z [36;1m# https://github.com/actions/download-artifact/blob/v8.0.1/src/download-artifact.ts[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7582283Z [36;1m# (see the `isSingleArtifactDownload || mergeMultiple || artifacts.length === 1`[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7583197Z [36;1m# branch). Remove this step once download-artifact stops flattening or[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7583640Z [36;1m# exposes an opt-out.[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7584030Z [36;1mexpected_count=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq '.group // [] | length')[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7584461Z [36;1mif [ "$expected_count" -eq 1 ]; then[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7584839Z [36;1m  group_id=$(echo "$EXPECTED_MATRIX" | jq -r '.group[0].id')[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7585331Z [36;1m  # Defence-in-depth: group_id is interpolated into a shell path. apm-prep[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7585874Z [36;1m  # produces a sanitised id today, but enforce a strict allowlist here so[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7586365Z [36;1m  # any future schema drift cannot smuggle traversal sequences.[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7586813Z [36;1m  if ! printf '%s' "$group_id" | grep -Eq '^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$'; then[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7587286Z [36;1m    echo "::error::unsafe group_id '$group_id' (must match ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$)"[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7587685Z [36;1m    exit 1[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7587894Z [36;1m  fi[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7588218Z [36;1m  group_dir="/tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles/${ARTIFACT_PREFIX}apm-${group_id}"[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7588617Z [36;1m  if [ ! -d "$group_dir" ]; then[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7589136Z [36;1m    mkdir -p "$group_dir"[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7589628Z [36;1m    find /tmp/gh-aw/apm-bundles -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -path "$group_dir" -exec mv {} "$group_dir/" \;[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:26.7590121Z [36;1m  fi[0m

2026-06-06T22:59:43.2199668Z [36;1m# Generate a secure random API key (360 bits of entropy, 40+ chars)[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:43.2200250Z [36;1m# Mask immediately to prevent timing vulnerabilities[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:43.2200799Z [36;1mAPI_KEY=$(openssl rand -base64 45 | tr -d '/+=')[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:43.2201220Z [36;1mecho "::add-mask::${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:43.2201551Z [36;1m[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:43.2201785Z [36;1mPORT=3001[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:43.2202044Z [36;1m[0m

2026-06-06T22:59:43.2203016Z [36;1m{[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:43.2203323Z [36;1m  echo "safe_outputs_api_key=${API_KEY}"[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:43.2203733Z [36;1m  echo "safe_outputs_port=${PORT}"[0m
2026-06-06T22:59:43.2204121Z [36;1m} >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"[0m

Suggested replacement for lines 164-170:\n\n```markdown\n## Plugin extraction\n\nWhen APM installs a plugin that contains `lspServers` in `plugin.json`\nor a `.lsp.json` file, the LSP servers are automatically extracted and\nwired into the install pipeline. The `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}` placeholder\nin server configs is replaced with the absolute plugin path for legacy\nClaude Code plugin compatibility.\n\nAPM accepts two `.lsp.json` layouts in a plugin:\n\n- **Flat** -- server names as top-level keys:\n  ```json\n  {\n    \"pyright\": { \"command\": \"pyright-langserver\", \"extensionToLanguage\": {\".py\": \"python\"} }\n  }\n  ```\n- **Wrapped** -- a `lspServers` envelope (standard Copilot/Claude format):\n  ```json\n  {\n    \"lspServers\": {\n      \"pyright\": { \"command\": \"pyright-langserver\", \"extensionToLanguage\": {\".py\": \"python\"} }\n    }\n  }\n  ```\n\nBoth layouts are treated identically after parsing.\n```"

The [Unreleased] section already has a '### Fixed' block (line 11); adding an entry there gives users who hit issue #1683 a changelog anchor. The project follows Keep a Changelog and includes attribution and issue references for every fix of this class. Omitting it makes the fix invisible in release notes.",
2026-06-06T23:05:59.2373864Z       "suggestion": "Add under `[Unreleased] ### Fixed`:\n\n```markdown\n- `apm install` now reads plugin `.lsp.json` files that use the\n  `{ \"lspServers\": { ... } }` envelope (the standard Copilot/Claude\n  format), in addition to the flat format where server names are\n  top-level keys. 2026-06-06T23:07:17.3672142Z       "rationale": "Input { 'lspServers': { 'lspServers': { 'command': 'x', 'extensionToLanguage': {'.py': 'python'} } } } unwraps once, leaving { 'lspServers': { ... } } as the servers dict. 'lspServers' satisfies _NAME_REGEX (all letters), so a server named 'lspServers' would be installed if its config is otherwise valid. This is not a bypass -- it goes through LSPDependency.validate(strict=True) like any other entry -- but the emergent behavior (a server named 'lspServers') is confusing. 2026-06-06T23:12:28.4814185Z         "principles": ["multi-harness-support", "devx", "portability-by-manifest"]
2026-06-06T23:12:28.4815013Z       }
2026-06-06T23:12:28.4817598Z     },
2026-06-06T23:12:28.4818401Z     {
2026-06-06T23:12:28.4824666Z       "severity": "nit",
2026-06-06T23:12:29.6759795Z       "summary": "lspServers: null in .lsp.json not tested; code falls through to flat format silently returning {'lspServers': None}",
2026-06-06T23:12:34.4768548Z       "rationale": "When _read_lsp_json parses { 'lspServers': null }, lsp_inner=None fails isinstance(None, dict) and the function falls through to return dict(data), returning {'lspServers': None}. _lsp_servers_to_apm_deps then encounters 'lspServers' as a server name with cfg=None, which fails isinstance(None, dict) and is silently skipped -- so no crash and no LSP dep is emitted. Behavior is acceptable, but this path has no test. The null case could arise from partially-written config files. Probed: grep of test_plugin_parser_lsp.py for 'null', 'None', 'lspServers.*None' returned no match. The existing test_unsupported_type_returns_empty only covers the case where the manifest key is an integer, not where _read_lsp_json returns a dict with a null-valued key. One parametrize entry on TestReadLspJson would cover this.",
2026-06-06T23:12:34.5343269Z       "file": "tests/unit/deps/test_plugin_parser_lsp.py",
2026-06-06T23:12:35.3408296Z       "suggestion": "Add to TestReadLspJson: def test_lsp_servers_null_falls_through_gracefully(self, tmp_path): lsp_file.write_text(json.dumps({'lspServers': None})); result = _read_lsp_json(lsp_file, ...); assert 'lspServers' in result and result['lspServers'] is None  # documents current behavior",
2026-06-06T23:12:35.3410834Z       "evidence": {
2026-06-06T23:12:35.3412584Z         "outcome": "missing",
2026-06-06T23:12:35.3414900Z         "tier": "unit",
2026-06-06T23:12:35.3429413Z         "test_file": "tests/unit/deps/test_plugin_parser_lsp.py",
2026-06-06T23:12:36.4860198Z         "test_name": "TestReadLspJson::test_lsp_servers_null_falls_through_gracefully",
2026-06-06T23:12:36.5476969Z         "assertion_excerpt": "result = _read_lsp_json(lsp_file, logger); assert result.get('lspServers') is None  # None key falls through; no crash",
2026-06-06T23:12:36.7539468Z         "proves": "When lspServers is present but null, _read_lsp_json does not crash and returns a predictable dict",
2026-06-06T23:12:36.7543418Z         "principles": ["devx"]
2026-06-06T23:12:36.7544295Z       }

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5684574Z   I'm now gathering the PR details to send to all agents: PR 1686 fixes the unwrapping of lspServers envelope in plugin .lsp.json files during install, with 90 additions and 2 deletions across 2 changed files.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5686056Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5688914Z   The core issue is that `_read_lsp_json()` was returning the parsed JSON as-is, so when plugins used the standard wrapper format with an "lspServers" key containing the actual server configs, that wrapper key was being treated as a server name itself. This caused the validation to fail because the nested dict (which contains the real servers) was missing the required "command" field, resulting in a misleading error message about the 'lspServers' dependency.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5692091Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5693254Z   The fix is straightforward: after parsing the JSON in `_read_lsp_jso... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2779 chars]

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5829906Z

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5830339Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5831345Z   1. **The fix itself**: The `_read_lsp_json()` function now properly unwraps the `lspServers` envelope, so the misleading `[!] Skipping invalid LSP server 'lspServers' from plugin 'my-plugin': LSP dependency 'lspServers' requires 'command'` error is eliminated. ✓
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5832581Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5832977Z   2. **Debug logging for the unwrap**: The fix silently unwraps without any `logger.debug()` call. 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5833428Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5834169Z   From a logging perspective, since `--verbose` mode is meant for agents and debuggers, adding a debug message when the envelope is detected and unwrapped would help diagnose issues. This is a minor gap.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5834979Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5835737Z   3. **Discrepancy between PR description and actual diff**: The PR body shows one version of the unwrapping logic, but the GitHub API diff shows a slightly different implementation that's still being written out.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5836581Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5839747Z   Adding validation logic... The other warning cases at lines 470, 474, and 477 are all properly handled. I'm looking at the test case `test_flat_server_named_lspservers_not_unwrapped` more carefully — it has a server named `lspServers` with values that are a string and a dict, not all dicts, so the type check correctly prevents unwrapping it as an envelope. The test confirms that `lspServers` is correctly preserved as a server name in the flat format edge case. Looking at the main CLI logging, silently unwrapping files without verbose output is fine since it corrects a misleading warning to silent success, though adding a debug log for `--verbose` mode would be a nice enhancement. The `_surface_warning` function is where the misleading warning originated, and the fix addresses that root cause.

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5851364Z       if isinstance(lsp_value, dict):
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5851750Z           servers = dict(lsp_value)  # Uses the dict directly - inline server defs
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5852153Z       elif isinstance(lsp_value, str):

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5854563Z   else:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5854780Z       # Fall back to auto-discovery: .lsp.json
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5855090Z       servers = {}
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5855325Z       candidate = plugin_path / ".lsp.json"
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5855727Z       if candidate.exists() and candidate.is_file() and not candidate.is_sym
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5856100Z   ```
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5856281Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5856503Z   Handling the different cases for lspServers...
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5856800Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5859637Z   When it's a string, I read from that file path through the fixed `_read_lsp_json` function. For auto-discovery, I check for a `.lsp.json` file and load it the same way. There's also the case where lspServers is a dict in the manifest itself, which gets used directly as server definitions, though I'm wondering if there could be nested structures like `{ "lspServers": { "lspServers": {...} } }` that I need to account for. So when the `.lsp.json` file is read, it properly unwraps the `lspServers` envelope to return just the server definitions. The edge case of having a nested `lspServers` key inside the manifest's inline format is theoretically possible but practically impossible and would just treat it as a server name anyway. Now I'm thinking through the architectural concerns around whether this fix is actually correct.

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5886492Z   **What this PR does (growth lens):**

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5889917Z

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5890818Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5891669Z   3. **Copilot/Claude alignment:** This is a real positioning win. APM now correctly handles the same format as both Copilot and Claude, reducing cognitive load for plugin authors already familiar with those tools.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5892726Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5893941Z   4. **Story angle:** "If your `.lsp.json` works with Copilot or Claude, it works with APM" — this is a compelling interoperability narrative. The LSP server management landed in 0.18.0, and this fix addresses a silent failure that was blocking the plugin ecosystem from fully leveraging it.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5895020Z

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5903607Z 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5903950Z [168] ✓ github-pull_request_read(method: get, owner: microsoft, pullNumber: 1686, repo: apm)
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5904317Z 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5904710Z [169] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive picture of the change. Let me summarize my findings as the devx-ux-expert:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5905197Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5905383Z   ## What the PR does
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5905596Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5906208Z   The PR fixes a bug where plugin `.lsp.json` files using the standard Copilot/Claude `{ "lspServers": { ... } }` envelope format were silently skipped with a confusing error:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5906887Z   ```
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5907332Z   [!] Skipping invalid LSP server 'lspServers' from plugin 'my-plugin': LSP dependency 'lspServers' requires 'command'
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5907850Z   ```
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5908030Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5908478Z   The fix detects and unwraps the `"lspServers"` envelope in `_read_lsp_json()`. The flat format continues to work unchanged.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5909009Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5909177Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5910486Z   The old error message was deeply confusing from a UX standpoint—it told users their perfectly valid standard format was "invalid" without explaining what was actually wrong or how to fix it. A plugin author following the canonical Copilot/Claude format would hit this error with no guidance on what to do next.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5911772Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5915899Z   The fix resolves this for the happy path by properly unwrapping the `lspServers` wrapper format, so servers register correctly and the misleading error disappears. The implementation is regression-safe since it tries to unwrap first, then falls back to treating the input as flat format, which handles the edge case where someone might have a server literally named `lspServers` in the flat format. This would cause those keys to fail validation since they're not valid server configurations themselves. It's an edge case where someone names a server `lspServers` in flat format, which would now be misinterpreted as the wrapped format instead. While this is an extremely unlikely scenario and the name is essentially reserved in the ecosystem, it's still a behavior change worth noting—though treating `lspServers` as a wrapper key aligns better with the dominant format convention anyway.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5919015Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5920060Z   When someone references an LSP file via string path in their manifest, the parser now correctly handles the wrapped format if that file uses it. For plugin authors, this is a meaningful improvement since the standard format just works without spe... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3838 chars]
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5921096Z 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5921465Z [170] ✓ $ grep -rn "\.lsp\.json\|lspServers" /home/runner/work/apm/apm/docs/src/ 2>/dev/null | head -30
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5921858Z

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5924403Z

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5924897Z 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5925199Z [174] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/de..., view_range: [545, 570])
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5925530Z

The "Plugin extraction" section in the docs mentions `.lsp.json` files but doesn't specify which format variants are acceptable, so plugin authors wouldn't know they can use either the flat or wrapped structure. Now that the code supports both, the documentation needs to be updated to clarify this.

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5946306Z [181] ◐ Now I have all the context I need. Let me summarize my findings from the cli-logging-expert perspective:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5946801Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5948271Z   1. **The fix correctly eliminates the misleading `[!] Skipping invalid LSP server 'lspServers'` warning** — The root cause was that `_read_lsp_json()` returned the outer dict verbatim, which caused `lspServers` to be treated as a server name with a dict value lacking a `command` field. The fix unwraps correctly. The misleading warning path is no longer triggered. ✓
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5949589Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5950105Z   2. **Silent success is appropriate** — When unwrapping succeeds silently, there's nothing actionable for the user. 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5950620Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5952734Z   3. **No debug logging on unwrap** — The fix doesn't include a debug-level log when the envelope is detected. Other similar parsers like `_read_lsp_file` log at warning level for errors but don't add debug logging for successful parse decisions either, so this is consistent with the existing pattern. That said, adding a debug message like "Detected lspServers envelope in [path]; unwrapping" would improve observability for diagnostics without cluttering normal output.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5954439Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5955546Z   4. **The `_surface_warning` function** — This routes warnings to both the stdlib logger and the console via `_rich_warning()`. The fix removes the erroneous call path through `_lsp_servers_to_apm_deps()`, so properly-wrapped files won't trigger spurious warnings anymore. ✓
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5956562Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5957178Z   5. **No existing debug logging in plugin_parser.py** — This is a pre-existing gap across the whole module, not something introduced by this change.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5957778Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5958288Z   6. **The actual guard is more conservative than described** — Looking at the GitHub diff, the conditional check is...
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5958792Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5958986Z   Validating dict structure...
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5959227Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5960348Z   The guard validates that all values in the `lspServers` dict are themselves dicts (server configs), which prevents accidentally treating a flat-format server named `lspServers` as the nested structure. This also protects against silently swallowing a server name that happens to collide with that key.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5961636Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5961858Z   So my findings break down into two categories:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5962146Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5962671Z   **Recommende... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 3051 chars]

2026-06-06T23:15:12.5997022Z [197] ◐ Now I have a thorough understanding of the PR and the surrounding code. Let me analyze the security implications:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5997540Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5997746Z   ## Security Analysis of PR #1686
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5998004Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5998195Z   ### What the PR does:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.5999546Z   The fix modifies `_read_lsp_json()` in `src/apm_cli/deps/plugin_parser.py` to detect and unwrap the `{ "lspServers": { ... } }` wrapper format in `.lsp.json` files. Previously, such files were silently failing because the code was treating the entire parsed dict (including the `lspServers` key) as a server mapping, so `"lspServers"` became a server name, which then failed validation.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6000913Z    
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6006517Z   Looking at the call chain, `_extract_lsp_servers()` invokes `_read_lsp_json()` during auto-discovery, which now returns the unwrapped inner dict when it detects the envelope format. This result flows through to `_lsp_servers_to_apm_deps()`, where each server entry is validated via `LSPDependency.from_dict()` with strict validation enabled. Now I'm examining whether this change introduces any security concerns around malicious envelope injection or expanding the attack surface. The validation pipeline then applies strict checks on each server config—name regex, path traversal guards, transport allowlist, and required fields—so even servers extracted from the wrapped format go through the same security gates as those from the flat format, meaning no validation bypass is introduced. regex check passes, so `lspServers` would be accepted as a valid server name with the nested config. If that inner config lacks required fields like `command` or `extensionToLanguage`, it gets skipped with a warning; otherwise it installs as a server named "lspServers". This isn't really a security issue since the name passes validation and the server still goes through all normal validation checks. The unwrapping only happens once though—if the inner dict also contains a `lspServers` key, that wouldn't trigger another level of unwrapping.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6011531Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6012102Z   Looking at what the validation actually covers, I see that `LSPDependency.validate()` checks the name ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 6119 chars]
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6012931Z 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6013318Z [198] ✓ view(path: /home/runner/work/apm/apm/src/apm_cli/ut...)

2026-06-06T23:15:12.6017000Z [203] ◐ Now I have all the information I need to compose my review. Let me assess my findings:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6017430Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6017831Z   **Finding 1 (recommended): Plugin extraction docs don't document both `.lsp.json` format variants**
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6018297Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6018765Z   The "Plugin extraction" section in `docs/src/content/docs/consumer/install-lsp-servers.md` (around line 164-170) says:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6019296Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6019932Z   > When APM installs a plugin that contains `lspServers` in `plugin.json` or a `.lsp.json` file, the LSP servers are automatically extracted and wired into the install pipeline.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6020641Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6020945Z   This mentions a `.lsp.json` file but doesn't say what format it can be in. 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6021319Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6023578Z   Before this fix, only the flat format with server names as top-level keys was supported. Now both the flat format and the wrapped `{ "lspServers": { ... } }` envelope work. Plugin authors using the wrapped format would have hit silent install failures previously, but that's now resolved. The documentation should be updated to clarify that `.lsp.json` supports both formats—the flat structure and the wrapped envelope format that matches the standard Copilot/Claude convention.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6025309Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6025545Z   **Finding 2 (recommended): CHANGELOG missing entry**
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6025856Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6026692Z   The `[Unreleased]` section needs a `Fixed` entry documenting this bug. This was a silent failure affecting plugin authors who shipped the standard `{ "lspServers": { ... } }` format, so it should be added to the existing `### Fixed` subsection.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6027610Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6027867Z   **Finding 3 (nit): docstring attribution of wrapped format**
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6028193Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6029083Z   The docstring in `_read_lsp_json` mentions that the wrapped format is standard in Copilot and Claude files, but this is slightly misleading since those are output files APM writes to, not input plugin files. The docstring should clarify this distinction.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6030047Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6031370Z   Actually, let me reconsider the accuracy here. APM does write to `.github/lsp.json` for Copilot CLI, and `~/.claude.json` uses the wrapped format for Claude's user scope, while Claude Code's project-scope uses the flat format. So the docstring's claim is accurate about what APM outputs, which is why plugin authors might ship that format... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2390 chars]
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6033040Z

2026-06-06T23:15:12.6034803Z 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6035197Z [207] ◐ Now I have a complete picture of this PR from a devx-ux-expert perspective. Let me compose my findings:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6035823Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6036024Z   ## Full picture
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6036225Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6037256Z   1. **The old error was user-hostile**: When a plugin ships `.lsp.json` with the `{ "lspServers": {...} }` wrapper (the format shown in the docs for Copilot/Claude), `apm install` would silently skip all servers and show `Skipping invalid LSP server 'lspServers'` - which gives zero actionable guidance.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6038350Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6038849Z   2. **The fix is correct and clean**: `_read_lsp_json()` now detects and unwraps the `lspServers` envelope. The flat format is regression-safe.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6039415Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6039587Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6040432Z   3. **The error message path still applies correctly**: The `Skipping invalid LSP server '{name}'` error now only fires for genuinely invalid servers missing required fields, not for the envelope confusion that was causing the original problem.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6041921Z   4. **Documentation gap**: The docs show the flat format for Claude Code's `.lsp.json` but Copilot CLI uses the wrapped format. After this fix, both formats work, but the docs don't clarify that either is acceptable.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6043262Z   5. **Unit tests cover the scenarios**: The PR includes tests for flat format, wrapped format, auto-discovery with wrapper, and end-to-end wrapped scenarios.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6044402Z   6. **Edge case with reserved key**: A server literally named `lspServers` in flat format would be silently mishandled, though this is extremely unlikely and worth noting in a code comment.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6045135Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6046978Z   The docs should be updated to show that plugin authors can use either the flat or wrapped `lspServers` format for their `.lsp.json` files. Now I'm structuring the JSON response with the required schema fields — persona, active status, findings array, and summary. I'll include two findings: one recommending the docs be updated to show both `.lsp.json` formats, and another noting that `lspServers` should be documented as a reserved key in flat-format files.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6048530Z 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6048762Z [208] ◆ Now I have everything I need. Let me compose my JSON verdict.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6049107Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6049285Z   ```json
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6049473Z   {
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6049681Z     "persona": "devx-ux-expert",

2026-06-06T23:15:12.6051522Z       {

2026-06-06T23:15:12.6135809Z 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6136175Z [231] ◐ Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the codebase. Let me compile my security analysis:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6136657Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6136850Z   ## Summary of findings
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6137073Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6137258Z   **The change:**
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6138425Z   A single 4-line addition to `_read_lsp_json()` that detects `{ "lspServers": { ... } }` wrapping and unwraps it. Previously, plugins using the wrapped format had their LSP servers silently skipped (because "lspServers" would fail as a server name with a dict config that lacks the required `command` and `extensionToLanguage` fields).
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6139626Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6139819Z   **Validation chain analysis:**
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6140301Z   1. `_read_lsp_json()` returns `dict(lsp_inner)` - a plain dict of server name → config
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6140707Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6140883Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6141151Z   2. `_extract_lsp_servers()` applies path substitution to the dict
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6141885Z   3. `_lsp_servers_to_apm_deps()` iterates through entries, applies a whitelist filter on keys, then validates each one through `LSPDependency.from_dict()`
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6143332Z   4. `LSPDependency.validate(strict=True)` enforces constraints on the name (regex and no `..` segments), command path (blocks `..` and handles encoding), workspace folder path, and transport type (restricted to an allowlist including stdio)
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6144439Z   5. Required fields are `command` and `extensionToLanguage` (must be a string-to-string mapping)
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6144903Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6146288Z   All unwrapped servers go through the same validation pipeline as flat-format servers—there's no special bypass. Double-wrapping like `{ "lspServers": { "lspServers": {...} } }` unwraps once, treats the inner dict as a server name, and if it's missing required fields, it gets skipped with a warning rather than creating a security issue.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6147522Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6149754Z   The `env` and `args` fields remain unvalidated, but that's pre-existing behavior unrelated to this PR—they only affect the LSP server process at runtime, not during installation. When `manifest["lspServers"]` points to a file path, the code reads and parses that file, which I'm examining for any additional validation concerns. Generating security review JSON... doesn't seem like a real security issue worth flagging. Looking at the test coverage, I've got unit tests for the core unwrapping logic, backward co... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 4897 chars]

2026-06-06T23:15:12.6175760Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6176077Z   Let me now compile my final security analysis and produce the JSON output.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6176460Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6176657Z   **Final Analysis Summary:**
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6176900Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6177939Z   1. **Malicious envelope injection / validation bypass:** The inner dict extracted by the new code flows directly into `_lsp_servers_to_apm_deps()` which routes every entry through `LSPDependency.from_dict()` → `validate(strict=True)`. This applies:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6178913Z      - Name format regex enforcement
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6179431Z      - `validate_path_segments()` on `command` (blocks `..`, handles percent-encoding)
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6179975Z      - `validate_path_segments()` on `workspaceFolder`
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6180319Z      - Transport allowlist (stdio|socket only)
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6180746Z      - Required fields: `command` + `extensionToLanguage` (must be dict[str,str])
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6181285Z      - Unrecognized keys are stripped (key allowlist in `_lsp_servers_to_apm_deps`)
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6181715Z      **No validation bypass introduced.**
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6181982Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6183137Z   2. **Double-wrapping / nested envelope abuse:** Attempting to nest the envelope structure like `{ "lspServers": { "lspServers": { "cmd": "evil", "ext": {...} } } }` unwraps only once, leaving the inner structure as a dict that fails validation since it doesn't match the expected LSPDependency schema.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6184226Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6185345Z   3. **Expanded attack surface:** The fix enables wrapped-format plugins to install their servers correctly when they pass validation, which is the intended behavior—not a security regression since malicious servers could already be injected via the flat format.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6186364Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6187107Z   4. **Validation gates apply uniformly:** Both formats go through the same checks: path segment validation for commands, regex for names, transport allowlist, and type enforcement for extensionToLanguage.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6187929Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6188751Z   5. **Pre-existing limitations:** The `env` and `args` fields aren't content-validated, which is unchanged and aligns with the LSP spec—editors pass these directly to the server process anyway.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6189513Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6189867Z   The security posture is unchanged; this fi... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 2055 chars]
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6190230Z

2026-06-06T23:15:12.6204051Z   {
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6204289Z     "persona": "supply-chain-security-expert",
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6204599Z     "active": true,
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6205219Z     "summary": "No security regression; unwrapped servers transit the existing LSPDependency validation chokepoint unmodified; threat model is unchanged.",
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6205877Z     "findings": [
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6206082Z       {
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6206276Z         "severity": "nit",
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6206938Z         "summary": "Double-wrapped .lsp.json produces a server named 'lspServers' that passes the name regex -- no real risk, but worth a test to document the behavior",
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6209316Z         "rationale": "Input { 'lspServers': { 'lspServers': { 'command': 'x', 'extensionToLanguage': {'.py': 'python'} } } } unwraps once, leaving { 'lspServers': { ... } } as the servers dict. 'lspServers' satisfies _NAME_REGEX (all letters), so a server named 'lspServers' would be installed if its config is otherwise valid. This is not a bypass -- it goes through LSPDependency.validate(strict=True) like any other entry -- but the emergent behavior (a server named 'lspServers') is confusing. A single test pinning this behavior would prevent future surprise.",
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6211302Z         "file": "tests/unit/deps/test_plugin_parser_lsp.py",

2026-06-06T23:15:12.6213394Z       }

2026-06-06T23:15:12.6248416Z [269] ◐ Now I have enough context to do my full review. Let me synthesize the findings.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6248828Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6249030Z   **Summary of what the PR does:**
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6249708Z   - Fixes a bug in `_read_lsp_json()` in `plugin_parser.py` where plugin `.lsp.json` files using the `{ "lspServers": { ... } }` wrapper format were silently mishandled.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6250641Z   - The fix adds a 4-line unwrap: detect `"lspServers"` key → if dict value → return the inner dict.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6251467Z   - Adds 4 tests covering: envelope unwrap, flat format regression, auto-discovery with envelope, and end-to-end with `_lsp_servers_to_apm_deps`.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6252086Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6252268Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6253331Z   - The fix is correct and minimal, mirroring the write-side logic in `_write_target_config()` that already handles envelope presence. It's a straightforward procedural function with a conditional guard at the right scope, no architectural patterns needed.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6254330Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6255477Z   - Looking at the target specs, Claude's `.lsp.json` uses flat format while Copilot's uses wrapped format with `"lspServers"` as the key. The fix allows plugins to support either variant, which aligns with the PR intent that plugins can target multiple runtimes, though there's a slight asymmetry from the target spec perspective.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6256694Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6257355Z   - There's a potential edge case where a plugin names a server `"lspServers"` in flat format, which could cause incorrect unwrapping, though this is unlikely given naming conventions.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6258087Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6258772Z   - The tests cover the new paths well with four new test cases, though importing logging inside test methods is unconventional. There's also a missing test case that would be worth adding.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6259565Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6260641Z   - When `lspServers` maps to a non-dict value like a string or list, the code falls through to returning the raw data dict, which then gets treated as a server name and skipped with a warning downstream. This behavior is reasonable and the warning is appropriate, but there's no explicit test for this edge case.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6261927Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6262187Z   - The docstring needs updating to reflect the new behavior.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6262661Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6263332Z   Now I'm planning the diagrams for this PR. Since it's a straightforward bug fix without class changes, I'll create a class diagram showing ... [truncated: showing first 2000 of 11044 chars]

2026-06-06T23:15:12.6274356Z      - copilot `.github/lsp.json`: `project_servers_key="lspServers"` (wrapped format on write)
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6275018Z   4. The plugin parser now correctly handles both formats when reading from a plugin's `.lsp.json` file.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6275502Z

The `parse_plugin_manifest` (which calls `_extract_lsp_servers` → `_lsp_servers_to_apm_deps`) is a critical step in the plugin install pipeline. This function generates `apm.yml` from `plugin.json`. The test `test_wrapped_lsp_json_produces_valid_deps` does NOT go through `parse_plugin_manifest` - it calls `_extract_lsp_servers` and `_lsp_servers_to_apm_deps` directly.
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6335659Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6335964Z   But does it need to? The function `parse_plugin_manifest` at lines 200-205:
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6336345Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6336521Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6336764Z   1. Calls `_extract_lsp_servers(plugin_path, manifest)` 
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6337387Z   2. If `lsp_servers` is non-empty, calls `_lsp_servers_to_apm_deps(lsp_servers, plugin_path)`
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6337866Z   3. Sets `manifest["_lsp_deps"] = lsp_deps`
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6338151Z   
2026-06-06T23:15:12.6338941Z   The test exercises steps 1 and 2 but not step 3 (the manifest injection). However, the bug fix is specifically in `_read_lsp_json` (step 1's sub-function), and the test covers steps 1 and 2, which is where the actual fix lives.

If auto-targeting fails (e.g., for schedule or workflow_dispatch triggers), the tool call will fail with an error.","type":["number","string"]},"pr":{"description":"Alias for item_number when targeting a pull request. Use this to confirm workflow completion and provide visibility when analysis is complete but no changes or outputs are required (e.g., 'No issues found', 'All checks passed'). This ensures the workflow produces human-visible output even when no other actions are taken.","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"message":{"description":"Status or completion message to log. Should explain what was analyzed and the outcome (e.g., 'Code review complete - no issues found', 'Analysis complete - all tests passing').","type":"string"},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"}},"required":["message"],"type":"object"},"name":"noop"},{"description":"Report that data or information needed to complete the task is not available. Use this when you cannot accomplish what was requested because required data, context, or information is missing.","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"alternatives":{"description":"Any workarounds, manual steps, or alternative approaches the user could take (max 256 characters).","type":"string"},"context":{"description":"Additional context about the missing data or where it should come from (max 256 characters).","type":"string"},"data_type":{"description":"Type or description of the missing data or information (max 128 characters). Be specific about what data is needed.","type":"string"},"integrity":{"description":"Trustworthiness level of the message source (e.g., \"low\", \"medium\", \"high\").","type":"string"},"reason":{"description":"Explanation of why this data is needed to complete the task (max 256 characters).","type":"string"},"secrecy":{"description":"Confidentiality level of the message content (e.g., \"public\", \"internal\", \"private\").","type":"string"}},"required":[],"type":"object"},"name":"missing_data"},{"description":"Signal that the task could not be completed due to an infrastructure or tool failure (e.g., MCP server crash, missing authentication, inaccessible repository). Use this when required tools or data are unavailable and the task cannot be meaningfully performed. This is distinct from noop (no action needed) — it indicates an active failure that prevented the task from running. The workflow framework will treat this as a failure signal even when the agent exits successfully.","inputSchema":{"additionalProperties":false,"properties":{"details":{"description":"Optional extended details or diagnostic context about the failure (max 65000 characters).","type":"string"},"reason":{"description":"A concise explanation of why the task could not be completed (max 1024 characters). Be specific about which tools or resources were unavailable.","type":"string"}},"required":["reason"],"type":"object"},"name":"report_incomplete"}]}}

Each thread contains logically grouped review comments made on the same code location during pull request reviews. 2026-06-06T23:15:12.8503438Z {"timestamp":"2026-06-06T23:00:24.957Z","event":"rpc_response","_schema":"rpc-message/v2","direction":"IN","server_id":"github","payload":{"id":1,"jsonrpc":"2.0","result":{"content":[{"text":"{\"number\":1686,\"title\":\"fix(install): unwrap lspServers envelope in plugin .lsp.json (closes #1683)\",\"body\":\"## TL;DR\\n\\nPlugin `.lsp.json` files using the `{ \\u0026#34;lspServers\\u0026#34;: { ... } }` wrapper format are now correctly unwrapped during install, instead of being silently skipped with a misleading validation error.\\n\\n## Problem\\n\\nWhen a plugin ships a `.lsp.json` using the standard wrapper format:\\n\\n```json\\n{\\n  \\u0026#34;lspServers\\u0026#34;: {\\n    \\u0026#34;my-lsp-server\\u0026#34;: {\\n      \\u0026#34;command\\u0026#34;: \\u0026#34;my-lsp\\u0026#34;,\\n      \\u0026#34;args\\u0026#34;: [\\u0026#34;--stdio\\u0026#34;],\\n      \\u0026#34;extensionToLanguage\\u0026#34;: { \\u0026#34;.ext\\u0026#34;: \\u0026#34;mylang\\u0026#34; }\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n```\\n\\n`_read_lsp_json()` returned the parsed JSON verbatim, causing `\\u0026#34;lspServers\\u0026#34;` to be treated as a server name. Its nested dict value (containing the actual servers) was then treated as that server\\u0026#39;s config -- which lacks a `command` field, producing:\\n\\n```\\n[!] Skipping invalid LSP server \\u0026#39;lspServers\\u0026#39; from plugin \\u0026#39;my-plugin\\u0026#39;: LSP dependency \\u0026#39;lspServers\\u0026#39; requires \\u0026#39;command\\u0026#39;\\n```\\n\\nThe real LSP servers were never written to `.github/lsp.json`.\\n\\n## Fix\\n\\nIn `_read_lsp_json()` (`src/apm_cli/deps/plugin_parser.py`), after parsing the JSON dict, detect whether it contains a `\\u0026#34;lspServers\\u0026#34;` key with a dict value. If so, return the inner dict (the actual server map). The flat format (server names as top-level keys) continues to work unchanged.\\n\\nThis mirrors the envelope handling already used by `LSPIntegrator._write_target_config()` when writing to Copilot and Claude config files.\\n\\n## Changes\\n\\n| File | Change |\\n|------|--------|\\n| `src/apm_cli/deps/plugin_parser.py` | 3-line envelope unwrap + updated docstring |\\n| `tests/unit/deps/test_plugin_parser_lsp.py` | 4 new tests |\\n\\n## Tests added\\n\\n- `test_unwraps_lsp_servers_envelope` -- wrapped format returns inner servers\\n- `test_flat_format_still_works` -- flat format regression guard\\n- `test_auto_discovery_with_lsp_servers_wrapper` -- auto-discovered `.lsp.json` with wrapper\\n- `test_wrapped_lsp_json_produces_valid_deps` -- end-to-end: wrapped file -\\u0026gt; valid deps\\n\\n## Validation\\n\\n- 20/20 tests pass in `tests/unit/deps/test_plugin_parser_lsp.py`\\n- `ruff check` + `ruff format --check` clean\\n- `pylint R0801` (duplication) clean\\n\\nFixes #1683\",\"state\":\"open\",\"draft\":false,\"merged\":false,\"mergeable_state\":\"blocked\",\"html_url\":\"https://github.com/microsoft/apm/pull/1686\",\"user\":{\"login\":\"sergio-sisternes-epam\",\"id\":207026618,\"profile_url\":\"https://github.com/sergio-sisternes-epam\",\"avatar_url\":\"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/207026618?v=4\"},\"labels\":[\"panel-review\"],\"requested_reviewers\":[\"danielmeppiel\"],\"head\":{\"ref\":\"sergio-sisternes-epam/fix-lsp-json-unwrap-lspservers-envelope\",\"sha\":\"[REDACTED]\",\"repo\":{\"full_name\":\"microsoft/apm\",\"description\":\"Agent Package Manager\"}},\"base\":{\"ref\":\"main\",\"sha\":\"[REDACTED]\",\"repo\":{\"full_name\":\"microsoft/apm\",\"description\":\"Agent Package Manager\"}},\"additions\":90,\"deletions\":2,\"changed_files\":2,\"commits\":1,\"created_at\":\"2026-06-06T22:56:19Z\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-06-06T22:59:21Z\"}","type":"text"}]}}}
